bins.name: Flexible Supply Arrangements
bins.desc: ECA strongly supports clearing pathways for flexible near-term power arrangements, including co-location, bring-your-own-power, and behind-the-meter resources. These approaches shift costs to private investors rather than ratepayers. ECA also supports faster interconnection timelines for loads that agree to curtailment or flexibility.
quotes: leveraging opportunities for these quickly emerging large loads to utilize flexible arrangements to secure and pay for their own power supply—such as the hybrid (sometimes called "co-location") arrangements described by the Secretary in the ANOPR, "bring your own power" models, utilization of behind the meter generation and microgrids, and the like—is also essential to addressing threats to customer affordability and ensuring that private investors, not captive ratepayers, take on the costs and risks of rapid large load growth.

ECA suggests a guideline requiring that transmission providers have in place a large load interconnection process that, under normal circumstances, can be completed in two years, with an exception for large load customers that provide flexibility or take other steps to limit the need for infrastructure upgrades (which, consistent with the seventh principle in the ANOPR, should be significantly faster).

ECA strongly agrees that large load customers that agree to be flexible or provide curtailment that reduces the need for extensive network upgrades should receive the benefit of faster interconnection timelines.

the study requirements and parameters do not later inappropriately restrict the ability of large load customers with on-site resources to provide services back to the grid, individually or through aggregation.

bins.name: Customer Affordability and Cost Allocation
bins.desc: ECA supports requiring large loads to pay 100% of assigned upgrade costs and favors cost causation principles broadly. The filing also calls for mandatory transparency on how interconnection costs flow into retail rates so states and customers can contest them.
quotes: leveraging opportunities for these quickly emerging large loads to utilize flexible arrangements to secure and pay for their own power supply—such as the hybrid (sometimes called "co-location") arrangements described by the Secretary in the ANOPR, "bring your own power" models, utilization of behind the meter generation and microgrids, and the like—is also essential to addressing threats to customer affordability and ensuring that private investors, not captive ratepayers, take on the costs and risks of rapid large load growth.

the Commission should require transmission providers to provide additional transparency of how costs incurred to interconnect to the FERC-jurisdictional transmission system will flow through to state retail tariffs.

ECA supports measures that ensure that all customers pay for the costs they cause and for the benefits they receive. A guideline requiring load and hybrid facilities to pay for 100% of the network upgrades they are assigned is sensible on its face and consistent with how generators are treated.

bins.name: Load and Generation Joint Study
bins.desc: ECA regards joint study of loads and their associated generation as one of the largest benefits Commission action can provide. It also supports broader transmission planning reform to better incorporate large load growth and associated generation development.
quotes: Aligning load interconnection and generator interconnection and better incorporating large load growth into transmission planning are the two biggest benefits action on the ANOPR can provide.
ECA strongly agrees with the need for study processes to more closely link new large loads with the new generation resources that they may wish to construct or contract.

bins.name: Co-location with Existing Generation
bins.desc: ECA sees co-location as a useful tool that can reduce delivery infrastructure costs, but warns that withdrawing existing generation from the market tightens supply and raises prices for other customers. ECA asks the Commission to require market and consumer impact studies beyond reliability-only analyses.
quotes: Co-location arrangements can be a valuable tool for large loads seeking to develop new power supplies or obtain enhanced reliability, and when structured appropriately, help protect existing customers from cost shifts or stranded cost risks by reducing the amount of delivery infrastructure that must be developed to serve large loads. However, co-location with existing generation comes with potential reliability and customer cost impacts for other customers, as taking that generation out of the market can tighten supply and raise prices.

bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Supported with State Guardrails
bins.desc: ECA agrees FERC has clear jurisdiction over large load transmission interconnections and that action is necessary. It asks the Commission to exercise that jurisdiction in a way that avoids reaching into retail rate matters and to require cost flow-through transparency for states.
quotes: ECA agrees with the Secretary's description in the ANOPR of the Commission's jurisdiction with respect to interconnection of large loads to the transmission system.

the Commission must exercise its jurisdiction to addresses current uncertainties holding back solutions to the challenges of serving large load growth and ensuring reliability and affordability, and it can do so in a way that recognizes the need for safeguards to avoid reaching into retail rate regulation.

bins.name: Joint Load and Generator Study Process
bins.desc: ECA strongly agrees that large loads and any generation they are developing should be studied together. Linking the two in interconnection and planning processes is one of the top benefits the Commission can deliver.
quotes: Aligning load interconnection and generator interconnection and better incorporating large load growth into transmission planning are the two biggest benefits action on the ANOPR can provide.
ECA strongly agrees with the need for study processes to more closely link new large loads with the new generation resources that they may wish to construct or contract.

bins.name: Study Deposits and Readiness Requirements
bins.desc: ECA endorses financial commitments, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties as tools to discipline load forecasts and deter speculative or duplicative interconnection requests.
quotes: study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties are critical components of the large load interconnection process. As the Secretary notes, these requirements and financial commitments are important to discipline load forecasts, avoid duplicative interconnection requests from the same large load customer for the same facility, and limit speculation by large load customers that drive inflated load forecasts.

bins.name: 20 MW Applicability Threshold
bins.desc: ECA finds 20 MW a reasonable starting threshold for triggering large load interconnection requirements, viewing it as a practical tool to limit regulatory burden without undermining jurisdictional reach.
quotes: ECA believes that 20 MW is a reasonable starting place for defining the new loads that should be subject to new requirements to have large load interconnection procedures and agreements in place.

bins.name: Hybrid Facility Rights and Study Parameters
bins.desc: ECA agrees hybrid facilities should be studied on actual planned operations. It cautions that study parameters should not block on-site or co-located resources from providing grid services in the future, and that co-location with existing generators requires market impact review beyond reliability studies.
quotes: Co-location arrangements can be a valuable tool for large loads seeking to develop new power supplies or obtain enhanced reliability, and when structured appropriately, help protect existing customers from cost shifts or stranded cost risks by reducing the amount of delivery infrastructure that must be developed to serve large loads. However, co-location with existing generation comes with potential reliability and customer cost impacts for other customers, as taking that generation out of the market can tighten supply and raise prices.

the study requirements and parameters do not later inappropriately restrict the ability of large load customers with on-site resources to provide services back to the grid, individually or through aggregation.

bins.name: Expedited Interconnection for Flexible Loads
bins.desc: ECA strongly supports faster interconnection for loads providing curtailment or flexibility. It proposes an added guideline requiring interconnection processes to be completed within two years under normal circumstances, with a faster track for flexible loads.
quotes: developing a full set of pro forma interconnection procedures and a pro forma agreement that can be applied nationwide will be exceedingly difficult if not impossible to accomplish by the April 30, 2026 date set by the Secretary for "final action."

ECA suggests that the Commission propose and seek comment on an additional guideline requiring that transmission providers demonstrate that their large load interconnection procedures will result in reasonable interconnection timelines.

ECA suggests a guideline requiring that transmission providers have in place a large load interconnection process that, under normal circumstances, can be completed in two years, with an exception for large load customers that provide flexibility or take other steps to limit the need for infrastructure upgrades (which, consistent with the seventh principle in the ANOPR, should be significantly faster).

ECA strongly agrees that large load customers that agree to be flexible or provide curtailment that reduces the need for extensive network upgrades should receive the benefit of faster interconnection timelines.

bins.name: Large Loads Pay 100% of Network Upgrades
bins.desc: ECA supports the principle that large load and hybrid customers pay for 100% of assigned network upgrades, consistent with generator interconnection rules. It also endorses crediting mechanisms so other benefiting customers share costs originally assigned to the large load.
quotes: ECA supports measures that ensure that all customers pay for the costs they cause and for the benefits they receive. A guideline requiring load and hybrid facilities to pay for 100% of the network upgrades they are assigned is sensible on its face and consistent with how generators are treated.

bins.name: PJM Challenges Warrant Commission Guidance
bins.desc: ECA notes PJM has struggled to define pathways for large load customers partly due to jurisdictional uncertainty and absent Commission guidance. PJM capacity prices have risen over 800% across the last three auctions while a clear integration plan remains lacking.
quotes: regions like PJM Interconnection, L.L.C. (PJM) and Southwest Power Pool, Inc. (SPP) have faced challenges in addressing large load growth and defining pathways for large load customers to obtain service while protecting other customers from reliability and cost risks, owing in part to jurisdictional uncertainties and a lack of guidance from the Commission on key implementation issues.

bins.name: SPP Large Load Integration Gaps
bins.desc: ECA cites SPP alongside PJM as a region where jurisdictional uncertainty and missing Commission guidance have hampered large load integration. SPP recently completed its stakeholder process but issues remain.
quotes: regions like PJM Interconnection, L.L.C. (PJM) and Southwest Power Pool, Inc. (SPP) have faced challenges in addressing large load growth and defining pathways for large load customers to obtain service while protecting other customers from reliability and cost risks, owing in part to jurisdictional uncertainties and a lack of guidance from the Commission on key implementation issues.

bins.name: Guidelines Over Pro Forma Rules
bins.desc: ECA's primary procedural recommendation is that the Commission adopt guidelines requiring transmission providers to file compliant procedures, rather than imposing uniform pro forma interconnection agreements. This approach handles regional variability, accommodates evolving state models, and can realistically be completed before the April 2026 deadline.
quotes: ECA strongly recommends that the Commission adopt a guidelines-based approach to establishing procedures for interconnection of large loads to the transmission system, in lieu of standardized pro forma procedures or agreements like those in place for generator interconnection.

developing a full set of pro forma interconnection procedures and a pro forma agreement that can be applied nationwide will be exceedingly difficult if not impossible to accomplish by the April 30, 2026 date set by the Secretary for "final action."

bins.name: Retail Cost Flow-Through Transparency
bins.desc: ECA asks the Commission to require transmission providers to show how interconnection costs flow into state retail tariffs and to identify forums where states and customers can contest those costs. This protects customers and gives states confidence that FERC action will not create hidden cost shifts.
quotes: the Commission should require transmission providers to provide additional transparency of how costs incurred to interconnect to the FERC-jurisdictional transmission system will flow through to state retail tariffs.

bins.name: Two-Year Timeline Guideline Proposed
bins.desc: ECA recommends adding a timeliness guideline not in the ANOPR's original principles, requiring that normal large load interconnections finish within two years and that flexible loads qualify for significantly faster treatment.
quotes: ECA suggests that the Commission propose and seek comment on an additional guideline requiring that transmission providers demonstrate that their large load interconnection procedures will result in reasonable interconnection timelines.

ECA suggests a guideline requiring that transmission providers have in place a large load interconnection process that, under normal circumstances, can be completed in two years, with an exception for large load customers that provide flexibility or take other steps to limit the need for infrastructure upgrades (which, consistent with the seventh principle in the ANOPR, should be significantly faster).
